content:
# Numbers Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 170–171 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_018_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_018_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0126

---

**1** And YHWH said to Aaron: "You and your sons and your father's house with you — you will bear the consequences of any violation against the sanctuary. And you and your sons with you will bear the consequences of any violation within your priesthood.

**2** And bring your kinsmen of the tribe of Levi — your father's tribe — close to you, so they may join you and serve you while you and your sons with you stand before the tent of the testimony.

**3** And they will carry out their duties to you and their duties to the entire tent. But they must not come near the sacred vessels or the altar — otherwise both they and you will die.

**4** And they will be joined to you and carry out the duties of the tent of meeting, for all the service of the tent. And no outsider may come near you.

**5** And you will carry out the duties of the sanctuary and the duties of the altar, so that there will never again be an outpouring of wrath against the Sons of Israel.

**6** And as for me — look, I myself have taken your kinsmen the Levites from among the Sons of Israel. They are a gift, given to YHWH, to carry out the service of the tent of meeting.

**7** And you and your sons with you will guard your priesthood in everything concerning the altar and everything behind the curtain. You will do the work. I am giving your priesthood to you as a gift of service, and any outsider who comes near will be put to death."

**8** And YHWH spoke to Aaron: "As for me — look, I am giving you charge over my contributions. From all the holy things of the Sons of Israel, I am giving them to you as a priestly portion, and to your sons, as a permanent allotment.

**9** This is what will be yours from the most holy things, kept from the fire: every offering they bring — whether grain offering, purification offering, or guilt offering — that they return to me, will be most holy, belonging to you and your sons.

**10** You will eat it in the most holy place. Every male may eat it. It will be holy to you.

**11** And this also is yours: the contribution of their gifts — including all the wave offerings of the Sons of Israel. I am giving them to you and to your sons and daughters with you as a permanent allotment. Everyone in your household who is ritually clean may eat them.

**12** All the finest olive oil, and all the finest new wine and grain — the firstfruits of what they give to YHWH — I am giving them to you.

**13** The first ripe produce of everything in their land, which they bring to YHWH, will be yours. Everyone in your household who is ritually clean may eat it.

**14** Everything in Israel that has been devoted to destruction will be yours.

**15** Every firstborn of any creature, whether human or animal, that is presented to YHWH will be yours. However, you must surely redeem the firstborn of a human, and you must redeem the firstborn of any unclean animal.

**16** And their redemption price — from one month old you will redeem them — will be set at five shekels of silver, by the sanctuary standard, which is twenty gerahs.

**17** But the firstborn of an ox, or the firstborn of a sheep, or the firstborn of a goat, you will not redeem. They are holy. You will splash their blood against the altar and turn their fat into smoke as a fire offering, a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**18** And their meat will be yours — just like the breast of the wave offering and the right thigh, it will be yours.

**19** All the sacred contributions that the Sons of Israel set apart for YHWH, I am giving to you and to your sons and daughters with you as a permanent allotment. It is a covenant of salt forever before YHWH — for you and your descendants with you."

**20** And YHWH said to Aaron: "In their land you will have no inheritance, and you will have no share among them. I am your share and your inheritance among the Sons of Israel.

**21** And to the sons of Levi — look, I am giving every tithe in Israel as their inheritance, in exchange for the service they perform — the service of the tent of meeting.

**22** And the Sons of Israel must no longer come near the tent of meeting, or they will bear the weight of sin and die.

**23** And the Levites — they will perform the service of the tent of meeting, and they will bear the consequences of their own failures. This is a permanent statute throughout your generations. And among the Sons of Israel they will receive no territorial inheritance.

**24** Because the tithe of the Sons of Israel — which they set apart as a contribution to YHWH — I am giving to the Levites as their inheritance. That is why I have said to them: Among the Sons of Israel they will receive no territorial inheritance."

**25** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying:

**26** "And you will speak to the Levites and say to them: When you receive the tithe from the Sons of Israel that I am giving you from them as your inheritance, you must set apart from it a contribution to YHWH — a tithe of the tithe.

**27** And your contribution will be credited to you as though it were grain from the threshing floor and juice from the winepress.

**28** In this way, you also will set apart a contribution to YHWH from all the tithes you receive from the Sons of Israel. And from it you will give YHWH's contribution to Aaron the priest.

**29** From all the gifts you receive, you will set apart every contribution due to YHWH — from the very best part of it, the portion that is to be consecrated from it."

**30** And you will say to them: "When you set apart the best part of it, it will be credited to the Levites as the produce of the threshing floor and the produce of the winepress.

**31** And you may eat it anywhere — you and your households — because it is your wages in exchange for your service at the tent of meeting.

**32** And you will bear no sin on account of it, provided you have set apart the best of it. And you must not profane the holy things of the Sons of Israel, or you will die."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "bear the consequences of any violation" (תשאו את עון המקדש / tis'u et 'awon hammikdash):** The Hebrew *nasa' 'awon* literally means "carry/bear iniquity." This is not simply "be responsible for" in a managerial sense — it means Aaron's line will absorb the lethal consequences when the sanctuary is profaned. The context follows directly from the Korah rebellion (Numbers 16–17), where unauthorized approach to the sacred resulted in mass death. YHWH is restructuring sacred access: Aaron's house becomes the buffer between a holy God and a vulnerable people. I rendered this as "bear the consequences of any violation" to capture the weight of absorbing guilt, not merely administrating it.

**Verse 2 — "join you" (וילוו עליך / weyillawu 'aleykha):** The verb *lawah* ("to join, attach") is the folk etymology of the name Levi itself (cf. Gen 29:34, where Leah says "now my husband will be joined to me"). The wordplay is deliberate: the Levites are etymologically the "joined ones," and here YHWH commands them to do exactly what their name describes. The Vaticanus LXX renders this with προστεθήτωσαν ("let them be added"), which loses the etymological resonance of the Hebrew.

**Verse 7 — "a gift of service" (עבדת מתנה / 'avodat mattanah):** The priesthood is framed as both labor and gift — a striking paradox. Aaron's line does not earn the priesthood; it is given. Yet it is also demanding work (*'avodah*). Dynamic equivalence demands we hold both poles: privilege and burden. The outsider (*zar*) who approaches will die — the boundary is lethal. This verse crystallizes the theology of the entire chapter: sacred access is simultaneously grace and mortal risk.

**Verse 9 — "kept from the fire" (מן האש / min ha'esh):** This phrase has generated debate. It likely refers to the portions of the most holy offerings that are not consumed on the altar fire — the part "kept back from the fire" that goes to the priests. The Vaticanus LXX reads ἀπὸ πάντων τῶν ἁγιασμάτων ("from all the consecrated things"), a slightly more general rendering. I follow the Masoretic text's specificity, rendering it as "kept from the fire" to preserve the concrete image of food snatched back from the altar flames for priestly consumption.

**Verse 15 — "you must surely redeem" (פדה תפדה / padoh tifdeh):** This is the infinitive absolute construction — *padoh tifdeh* — which intensifies the verbal action. The doubling conveys emphatic obligation: redemption of human firstborn is not optional. Every Israelite firstborn belongs to YHWH (recalling the Passover, Exod 13:2), but the child's life is bought back through payment. I rendered the intensification as "you must surely redeem" to preserve the doubled force of the Hebrew infinitive absolute.

**Verse 19 — "a covenant of salt" (ברית מלח / berit melakh):** Salt preserves and endures. A "salt covenant" is an unbreakable, perpetual agreement (cf. 2 Chr 13:5, where Davidic kingship is also called a salt covenant). In the ancient Near East, sharing salt at a meal sealed a bond. The priestly portions are not temporary rations — they are a permanent covenant right. The LXX renders this as νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("an eternal statute"), flattening the salt metaphor. I preserved "covenant of salt" because the image itself does theological work: it communicates permanence, preservation, and shared table fellowship between YHWH and his priests.

**Verse 20 — "I am your share and your inheritance" (אני חלקך ונחלתך / 'ani khelqekha wenakhalatekha):** This is one of the most theologically dense statements in Torah. While every other tribe receives territory — land, soil, vineyards — the priests receive God himself. The words *kheleq* (portion) and *nakhalah* (inheritance) are the standard vocabulary for land allotment. YHWH is claiming to be their real estate. This is not abstract spirituality but a concrete economic statement: the priests live on sacred food because their "land" is YHWH. The Vaticanus confirms the reading straightforwardly: ἐγὼ μερίς σου καὶ κληρονομία σου.

**Verse 21 — "every tithe" (כל מעשר / kol ma'aser):** The Levitical tithe system is established here as the economic counterpart to the theological claim of verse 20. Because the Levites have no land, they receive one-tenth of what the land produces through everyone else. The tithe is not charity — it is their inheritance equivalent. The word *ma'aser* derives from *'eser* (ten), making "tithe" (tenth) the correct dynamic rendering.

**Verse 26 — "a tithe of the tithe" (מעשר מן המעשר / ma'aser min hamma'aser):** The Levites themselves must tithe what they receive. This creates a cascading economy of sacred giving: Israel gives a tenth to the Levites, and the Levites give a tenth of that tenth to the Aaronic priests. No one in the system is exempt from the discipline of offering back. The phrase is elegantly recursive in Hebrew, and I preserved the doubling — "a tithe of the tithe" — because the repetition itself communicates the theological principle.

**Verse 27 — "as though it were grain from the threshing floor" (כדגן מן הגרן / kidgan min haggoren):** Since the Levites have no farmland, they have no literal harvest to offer. YHWH graciously credits their tithe-of-the-tithe as if it were agricultural produce. The comparison to threshing floor and winepress normalizes the Levites' offering — they are treated as full participants in the agrarian economy of worship despite owning no fields. This is a remarkable act of divine accommodation to their landless status.

**Verse 32 — "you must not profane the holy things" (לא תחללו את קדשי בני ישראל / lo tekhalelu et qodshey beney Yisra'el):** The chapter ends with a death warning. The verb *khalal* ("profane, pollute") is the opposite of *qadash* ("make holy"). If the Levites fail to set apart the best portion, they desecrate what Israel has consecrated — and the penalty is death. The chapter thus closes as it opened: sacred service is simultaneously gift and mortal responsibility. The entire structure of Numbers 18 forms an inclusio around this theme — privilege bounded by peril.
